title: Injection of orbital angular momentum into transition metals from first-principles
abstract: We use quantum mechanical scattering calculations implemented in a basis of tight-binding muffin-tin orbitals to calculate nonequilibrium spin and orbital currents in transition metals with a view to understanding the length scale on which they decay. In the case of spin currents, the relaxation length, called the spin-flip diffusion length, is reasonably well understood. We apply our experience with spin currents to study orbitally-polarized currents and find that they behave qualitatively differently. Upon injection from a lead, orbital currents decay within a few atomic layers contradicting the current interpretation of experimental results which appear to show exponential decay on the length scale of the spin-flip diffusion length and longer. When spin-orbit coupling is included, the injected orbital current is partially converted into a spin current within a few atomic layers. This insight provides a new perspective on the physics of the orbital Hall effect.
summary: The study uses first-principles calculations to show that injected orbital angular momentum currents in transition metals decay much more rapidly (within a few atomic layers) than previously thought. It reveals that spin-orbit coupling plays a crucial role in converting these short-lived orbital currents into longer-lived spin currents.
main_problem: Investigating the decay length scale of injected orbital angular momentum (OAM) currents in transition metals and addressing the discrepancy between theoretical predictions and experimental interpretations.
main_result: Orbital currents decay rapidly within a few atomic layers upon injection, unlike spin currents which have longer diffusion lengths; however, in the presence of spin-orbit coupling, injected orbital current is partially converted into a spin current.
method: First-principles quantum mechanical scattering calculations using a tight-binding muffin-tin orbital (TB-MTO) basis and Ando's Wave Function Matching scheme.
model_or_system: Transition metals (including Pt, Ti, V, Cr, and Cu) modeled as a scattering region sandwiched between two semi-infinite leads, incorporating spin and orbital Zeeman terms to create polarized leads.
key_observables: Charge, spin, and orbital currents (j_c, j_s, j_l); orbital Hall conductivity (OHC); spin Hall conductivity (SHC); spin-flip and orbital diffusion lengths.
important_parameters: Spin-orbit coupling (SOC) strength, thermal lattice disorder (temperature), and spin-flip diffusion length (l_sf).
main_assumptions: The orbital diffusion length is assumed to be driven by electronic hopping; SOC is sometimes switched off in the scattering region to isolate orbital effects; the orbital Zeeman term is used as a proxy for orbital polarization.
figures_summary: Schematic of the L|S|R geometry for current generation; plots showing the decay of injected orbital polarization in Pt, Cr, and V; and visualization of current density in the leads.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of OAM decay, describes the TB-MTO scattering formalism, presents results for various transition metals (Pt, 3d metals), discusses the role of SOC and disorder, and concludes with implications for orbitronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the left-lead|scattering-region|right-lead (L|S|R) geometry. As a result of the spin/orbital Hall effects (SHE/OHE), a charge current jc originating in the leads par- allel to the z direction gives rise to a current jX of spin angu- lar momentum (SAM, X = s) or orbital angular momentum (OAM, X = l) perpendicular to it. Alternatively, by includ- ing a magnetic field in the left lead that couples to either the SAM or the OAM, jc can be polarized directly. Adapted with permission from Rang and Kelly [17]. Copyright (2025) by the American Physical Society.

FIG. 2. Fermi energy dependence of (left-hand axis) the or- bital (spin) Hall conductivity for bulk Pt at room tempera- ture, meaning the atoms are randomly displaced from equilib- rium by a Gaussian distribution with root mean square value ∆= 0.067˚A. The DOS is shown with respect to the right- hand axis. Reprinted with permission from Rang and Kelly [17]. Copyright (2025) by the American Physical Society.

FIG. 3. Exponential decay of a spin-polarized current injected from a ballistic Pt lead into thermally disordered Pt. To vi- sualize the current in the lead, the interface between ballistic (pink) and diffusive Pt is displaced into the scattering region to z = zI [54]. Reprinted with permission from Rang and Kelly [37]. Copyright (2024) by the American Physical Soci- ety.

FIG. 4. Injection of a current of OAM polarized in the z direction, jz lz, from a spin-polarized (in the z direction) Pt lead (shaded rose) into Pt without (left column) and with (right column) thermal lattice disorder corresponding to T = 300 K.

FIG. 5. Injection of a current of SAM polarized in the z direction, jz sz, from a spin-polarized (in the z direction) Pt lead (shaded rose) into Pt without (left column) and with (left column) thermal lattice disorder corresponding to T = 300 K.
